answer:
In Iqbal's *Sair-e-Falak*, a moment of cosmic vision arrives when the speaker, after passing through the stars, Paradise, and Tooba's bough, beholds a frozen void beyond and learns its nature from the angel Surosh: Hell is not innately fiery but an empty realm where damned souls supply their own embers from earthly deeds.[^1]

In *The Wanderer*, the lone exile's earthbound reverie yields a cosmic vision of inevitable decay when he contemplates ancient ruined walls—remnants of giants' work—and intuits how the Shaper of men has wasted the world, leaving it ghostly and stripped bare under Wyrd's decree, as evoked in the *ubi sunt* lament: "Where has the horse gone? Where has the warrior gone? Where the treasure-giver?"[^2]

Iqbal's speaker is given more to see: a literal ascent through astral and paradisiacal realms to the structure of Hell itself dwarfs the Wanderer's stationary, wintry survey of middle-earth's ruins (though both grasp a divine order behind mutability).

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 18 (*Sair-e-Falak*), [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/18-sair-e-falak/translation/#p-9d0a05).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
